title: verify returns Result<bool, Error> — bool conflates two distinct conditions
description: What the code does: EccKey::verify returns Ok(true) for valid signature, Ok(false) for invalid signature, Err(_) for TPM errors. What idiomatic Rust would do: the three-outcome case is a natural fit for a three-variant type: enum VerifyResult { Valid, Invalid, Error(Error) } or, more ergonomically, return Result<(), Error> where Ok(()) means valid and a dedicated Error::SignatureInvalid means invalid. The current bool return means callers can accidentally ignore the bool (let _ = key.verify(...)?) and still get an Ok result, silently ignoring an invalid signature. This is a real footgun: a caller using '?' on a Result<bool> will propagate Err but will drop the false case silently if they write 'key.verify(h, s)?; // assume true'. Why it matters: this is a cryptographic API — treating Ok(false) as Ok(true) is a security bypass.
notes: Requires breaking API change before 1.0: change Result<bool, Error> to Result<(), Error> with Error::SignatureInvalid variant, or a three-variant VerifyResult enum. This is the most important safety-critical API issue to fix before publish. Defer to pre-1.0.
